news_summary: Today’s news highlights efforts to address systemic challenges undermining human rights and development, the empowerment of individuals overcoming online misogyny to advance their careers, and UN Women’s committed presence to support rights amid crises in Afghanistan.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité d'aujourd'hui met en lumière les efforts déployés pour relever les défis systémiques qui portent atteinte aux droits humains et au développement, l'autonomisation des personnes qui surmontent la misogynie en ligne pour faire avancer leur carrière, ainsi que l'engagement sans faille d'ONU Femmes à défendre les droits humains dans le contexte des crises en Afghanistan.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy destacan las iniciativas para hacer frente a los retos sistémicos que socavan los derechos humanos y el desarrollo, el empoderamiento de las personas que superan la misoginia en Internet para avanzar en sus carreras, y la firme presencia de ONU Mujeres en la defensa de los derechos en medio de la crisis en Afganistán.

articles.title: UN: Global Tax System Undermines Rights, Development
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              The United Nations Headquarters in New York City, US, July 16, 2024.
                    © 2024 Jakub Porzycki/NurPhoto via AP Photo
          
    


  
(New York) – UN member countries should work to align the emerging United Nations tax convention with international human rights law to achieve its objective of advancing sustainable development, seven human rights groups said today in a submission to the Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee. The submission proposes edits to the draft text to incorporate these standards and guidance into the draft convention.The groups are Amnesty International; the Center for Economic and Social Rights; Dejusticia; the Global Initiative for Economic, Social, and Cultural Rights; Human Rights Watch; the Initiative for Human Rights in Fiscal Policy; and the Tax Justice Network.“The current outdated and patchwork global tax system is driving an extreme concentration of wealth that is depriving most governments of money that could fund rights like health, education, and social security,” said Sarah Saadoun, senior advisor on economic inequality at Human Rights Watch said. “Aligning the tax convention with human rights is the best way to provide a strong foundation for a fairer global tax system that could let governments improve people’s lives.”Negotiations for a first UN tax treaty began in 2025, with a draft to be submitted to the UN General Assembly in 2027. The United States withdrew during the first round of negotiations in February 2025, but dozens of other governments, including European Union members and other major economies, have been actively engaging in negotiations.The countries agreed to make sustainable development the explicit objective of the new UN tax convention. Human rights can provide standards and guidance to help ensure that the convention achieves this objective. International human rights law requires governments to take steps, “to the maximum of [their] available resources,” to progressively deliver the full realization of economic, social and cultural rights—such as education, health care, and social security—both individually and through international cooperation and assistance. Practically speaking, the effective protection of other human rights depends on having adequate government resources as well.Numerous UN human rights mechanisms have underscored that a fair international tax system is critical to any hopes of enabling governments to mobilize adequate domestic resources to fulfill human rights.The debates at the heart of the treaty negotiations have enormous implications for rights. Governments lose about US $500 billion globally each year to tax abuse, according to the Tax Justice Network. The UN Tax Convention could help generate significant income for governments by, for example, curbing tax evasion and avoidance, preventing companies from shifting profits to tax havens, and giving governments the right to tax companies whose profits are unjustifiably put out of reach under current rules.“Tax is one of the most powerful tools governments have to realise human rights”, said Sergio Chaparro, international policy and advocacy lead at the Tax Justice Network. “Global tax rules have restricted the ability of countries to serve broader aims such as the fulfillment of human rights. The UN tax convention offers a historic opportunity to change course.”Governments would be able to mobilize significant new revenue under a fairer global tax system, which would vastly improve their ability to fund public services and social security systems, while reducing their reliance on taxes that disproportionately harm people with lower incomes, the groups said.“Many of the same governments that claim to support human rights are fighting the hardest to maintain a tax system that deprives governments of the revenues they need to fulfill rights,” said Camila Barretto Maia, executive director of the Global Initiative for Economic, Social and Cultural Rights.Research in countries such as Sri Lanka has shown that the global tax system undermines governments’ ability to fund human rights. Nearly half the world’s population—3.6 billion people—live in poverty, defined as less that $6.85 per day, and around the same number have no access to any social security benefits. More than half—4.6 billion people—still lack access to essential health services. Fixing the current tax system is critical to ensure universal support for these rights, the groups said.“Human rights and fair tax cooperation must go hand in hand. Embedding human rights in the convention can strengthen the negotiations and help a global tax architecture that currently enables abuse and deprives governments of the resources needed to realise economic, social and cultural rights,” said Maria Ron Balsera, Executive Director of at the Center for Economic and Social Rights.It is also essential to the realisation of civil and political rights. Anger over economic inequality and elite capture is fueling the rise of authoritarianism in some countries, and motivating protesters to go into the streets to demand change in others.“At a time when international cooperation and assistance is needed more than ever,” said Riva Jalipa, researcher and advisor with Amnesty International, “The UN tax convention provides an historic opportunity for more transparency, accountability and the participation of states on an equal footing to rewrite a more equal global tax system.”
articles.content:
Click to expand Image The United Nations Headquarters in New York City, US, July 16, 2024. © 2024 Jakub Porzycki/NurPhoto via AP Photo

(New York) – UN member countries should work to align the emerging United Nations tax convention with international human rights law to achieve its objective of advancing sustainable development, seven human rights groups said today in a submission to the Intergovernmental Negotiating Committee. The submission proposes edits to the draft text to incorporate these standards and guidance into the draft convention.

The groups are Amnesty International; the Center for Economic and Social Rights; Dejusticia; the Global Initiative for Economic, Social, and Cultural Rights; Human Rights Watch; the Initiative for Human Rights in Fiscal Policy; and the Tax Justice Network.

“The current outdated and patchwork global tax system is driving an extreme concentration of wealth that is depriving most governments of money that could fund rights like health, education, and social security,” said Sarah Saadoun, senior advisor on economic inequality at Human Rights Watch said. “Aligning the tax convention with human rights is the best way to provide a strong foundation for a fairer global tax system that could let governments improve people’s lives.”

Negotiations for a first UN tax treaty began in 2025, with a draft to be submitted to the UN General Assembly in 2027. The United States withdrew during the first round of negotiations in February 2025, but dozens of other governments, including European Union members and other major economies, have been actively engaging in negotiations.

The countries agreed to make sustainable development the explicit objective of the new UN tax convention. Human rights can provide standards and guidance to help ensure that the convention achieves this objective. International human rights law requires governments to take steps, “to the maximum of [their] available resources,” to progressively deliver the full realization of economic, social and cultural rights—such as education, health care, and social security—both individually and through international cooperation and assistance. Practically speaking, the effective protection of other human rights depends on having adequate government resources as well.

Numerous UN human rights mechanisms have underscored that a fair international tax system is critical to any hopes of enabling governments to mobilize adequate domestic resources to fulfill human rights.

The debates at the heart of the treaty negotiations have enormous implications for rights. Governments lose about US $500 billion globally each year to tax abuse, according to the Tax Justice Network. The UN Tax Convention could help generate significant income for governments by, for example, curbing tax evasion and avoidance, preventing companies from shifting profits to tax havens, and giving governments the right to tax companies whose profits are unjustifiably put out of reach under current rules.

“Tax is one of the most powerful tools governments have to realise human rights”, said Sergio Chaparro, international policy and advocacy lead at the Tax Justice Network. “Global tax rules have restricted the ability of countries to serve broader aims such as the fulfillment of human rights. The UN tax convention offers a historic opportunity to change course.”

Governments would be able to mobilize significant new revenue under a fairer global tax system, which would vastly improve their ability to fund public services and social security systems, while reducing their reliance on taxes that disproportionately harm people with lower incomes, the groups said.

“Many of the same governments that claim to support human rights are fighting the hardest to maintain a tax system that deprives governments of the revenues they need to fulfill rights,” said Camila Barretto Maia, executive director of the Global Initiative for Economic, Social and Cultural Rights.

Research in countries such as Sri Lanka has shown that the global tax system undermines governments’ ability to fund human rights. Nearly half the world’s population—3.6 billion people—live in poverty, defined as less that $6.85 per day, and around the same number have no access to any social security benefits. More than half—4.6 billion people—still lack access to essential health services. Fixing the current tax system is critical to ensure universal support for these rights, the groups said.

“Human rights and fair tax cooperation must go hand in hand. Embedding human rights in the convention can strengthen the negotiations and help a global tax architecture that currently enables abuse and deprives governments of the resources needed to realise economic, social and cultural rights,” said Maria Ron Balsera, Executive Director of at the Center for Economic and Social Rights.

It is also essential to the realisation of civil and political rights. Anger over economic inequality and elite capture is fueling the rise of authoritarianism in some countries, and motivating protesters to go into the streets to demand change in others.

“At a time when international cooperation and assistance is needed more than ever,” said Riva Jalipa, researcher and advisor with Amnesty International, “The UN tax convention provides an historic opportunity for more transparency, accountability and the participation of states on an equal footing to rewrite a more equal global tax system.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on an ongoing international effort to reform the global tax system through a UN tax convention aligned with human rights law, aiming to generate significant government revenue to fund essential public services and social rights. This has broad, meaningful implications for billions of people worldwide by addressing economic inequality and enabling sustainable development, with detailed context on the negotiations, stakeholders, and expected positive impacts.
articles.personality_title: UN talks aim to reshape global tax system to support human rights and development
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The world’s tax system is old and complicated. It allows rich people and companies to avoid paying taxes, which means many governments don’t get enough money to pay for things like schools, hospitals, and social help. This makes it harder for countries to support the rights of their people.

**What happened** – In 2025, countries began working on a new UN tax treaty to fix this problem. Seven human rights groups, including Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch, asked the UN to make sure this new tax treaty follows international human rights laws. They want the treaty to help governments have the money they need to provide important services and protect people’s rights.

**Impact** – If the treaty works, it could stop companies from hiding profits in tax havens and reduce tax cheating. This would give governments more money—about $500 billion lost each year—to spend on health care, education, and social security. This is important because billions of people still live in poverty and lack basic services. The treaty could also help reduce unfair taxes that hurt poor people the most.

**What's next step** – The draft treaty will be ready by 2027 and discussed by many countries. Even though the United States left the talks early, many others, including European countries, are involved. If they agree, the treaty could change global tax rules and help countries better support their people’s rights.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN is working on a new tax treaty to make the global tax system fairer and help governments fund services that protect human rights and support sustainable development.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les négociations de l’ONU visent à réformer le système fiscal mondial pour soutenir les droits humains et le développement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le système fiscal mondial est ancien et compliqué. Il permet aux riches et aux entreprises d’éviter de payer des impôts, ce qui fait que de nombreux gouvernements manquent d’argent pour financer des services comme les écoles, les hôpitaux et l’aide sociale. Cela rend plus difficile pour les pays de protéger les droits de leurs habitants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, les pays ont commencé à travailler sur un nouveau traité fiscal de l’ONU pour résoudre ce problème. Sept groupes de défense des droits humains, dont Amnesty International et Human Rights Watch, ont demandé à l’ONU de s’assurer que ce traité respecte les lois internationales sur les droits humains. Ils veulent que le traité aide les gouvernements à avoir l’argent nécessaire pour fournir des services importants et protéger les droits des personnes.

**Impact** – Si le traité fonctionne, il pourrait empêcher les entreprises de cacher leurs profits dans des paradis fiscaux et réduire la fraude fiscale. Cela donnerait plus d’argent aux gouvernements—environ 500 milliards de dollars perdus chaque année—pour dépenser en santé, éducation et sécurité sociale. C’est important car des milliards de personnes vivent encore dans la pauvreté et n’ont pas accès aux services de base. Le traité pourrait aussi réduire les impôts injustes qui touchent le plus les pauvres.

**Prochaine étape** – Le projet de traité sera prêt en 2027 et discuté par de nombreux pays. Bien que les États-Unis se soient retirés tôt, beaucoup d’autres, dont des pays européens, participent aux négociations. S’ils s’accordent, le traité pourrait changer les règles fiscales mondiales et aider les pays à mieux soutenir les droits de leurs populations.

**Message clé en une phrase** – L’ONU travaille sur un nouveau traité fiscal pour rendre le système fiscal mondial plus juste et aider les gouvernements à financer des services qui protègent les droits humains et soutiennent le développement durable.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU busca reformar el sistema fiscal global para apoyar los derechos humanos y el desarrollo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El sistema fiscal mundial es antiguo y complicado. Permite que personas ricas y empresas eviten pagar impuestos, lo que hace que muchos gobiernos no tengan suficiente dinero para pagar servicios como escuelas, hospitales y ayuda social. Esto dificulta que los países protejan los derechos de su gente.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, los países comenzaron a trabajar en un nuevo tratado fiscal de la ONU para solucionar este problema. Siete grupos de derechos humanos, incluyendo Amnistía Internacional y Human Rights Watch, pidieron a la ONU que este nuevo tratado siga las leyes internacionales de derechos humanos. Quieren que el tratado ayude a los gobiernos a obtener el dinero que necesitan para ofrecer servicios importantes y proteger los derechos de las personas.

**Impacto** – Si el tratado funciona, podría impedir que las empresas oculten ganancias en paraísos fiscales y reducir la evasión fiscal. Esto daría a los gobiernos más dinero—unos 500 mil millones de dólares que se pierden cada año—para gastar en salud, educación y seguridad social. Esto es importante porque miles de millones de personas aún viven en pobreza y no tienen acceso a servicios básicos. El tratado también podría reducir impuestos injustos que afectan más a los pobres.

**Próximo paso** – El borrador del tratado estará listo para 2027 y será discutido por muchos países. Aunque Estados Unidos se retiró temprano, muchos otros, incluidos países europeos, están participando. Si llegan a un acuerdo, el tratado podría cambiar las reglas fiscales globales y ayudar a los países a apoyar mejor los derechos de sus ciudadanos.

**Mensaje clave en una frase** – La ONU está trabajando en un nuevo tratado fiscal para hacer el sistema fiscal global más justo y ayudar a los gobiernos a financiar servicios que protejan los derechos humanos y apoyen el desarrollo sostenible.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a balanced scale where one side holds stylized golden coins symbolizing global wealth, and the other side holds vibrant icons representing human rights essentials—books for education, a heart for health, and a shield for social security—set against a softly glowing backdrop of interconnected puzzle pieces shaped like continents, symbolizing international cooperation and a fair global tax system.

articles.title: How I turned online misogyny about my PhD into momentum for my career
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-025-04129-7A simple celebratory post about completing my PhD went viral for all the wrong reasons. Here’s how I managed the backlash and used the attention to promote my research.
articles.content:
Juliet Turner was harassed on social media after posting about the successful completion of her PhD.Credit: Simon Kershenbaum

After devoting nearly four years to my PhD research on how insects cooperate and divide labour, I thought nothing of posting a photo on social media to celebrate passing the final oral examination of my degree. I wore a traditional academic gown, smiling in front of the golden limestone walls of New College at the University of Oxford, UK. “You can call me Doctor,” I wrote, punctuated with a sunglasses emoji. Maybe a bit cheeky, but harmless. Or so I thought.

In just a few hours, a screenshot of my post had been circulated widely on the social-media platform X, accompanied by comments questioning my competence and the wisdom of my life choices, rating my physical appearance and speculating about my fertility. One read, “they just handing doctorates out to anyone now” and another suggested that I would have spent my time better by having four babies instead of studying. One such post now has more than 21 million views.

I’d seen this kind of hostility before: a year earlier, an academic at the University of Cambridge, UK, had posted about her thesis on the politics of smell and was subjected to similar derision. Naively, I assumed that by working in a scientific field, I was safe. It quickly became clear that the common denominator between people targeted for ‘daring’ to post about their academic accomplishments was not their discipline, but their gender.

Meeting misogyny head-on

When the backlash escalated, I had three options: stay silent, delete the post or confront the comments. I decided on the last, mostly because these comments and beliefs just seemed so bizarre and entertaining to me that I felt I should share them with the world. It was so difficult to take them seriously that I started finding them funny.

I began reposting the best examples with commentary. Here are some highlights.

“I’m sure [your thesis] keeps you warm at night while your eggs shrivel and die.”

“If you follow an academic career, you will be unlikely to ever have or raise children — thus billions of years of evolution and suffering ends with you and your thesis.”

“Congrats on successfully becoming a biologist, but failing at biology. You are 30 years old with no husband or kids — a genetic dead end in an unbroken line of succession from your ancestors since the beginning of time. But hey, at least you have your cats to keep you company!”

Is the Internet bad for you? Huge study reveals surprise effect on well-being

For the record, not that it really matters, I am 27, live with my partner and am the product of an academic family. The idea that earning a doctorate means resigning yourself to a life of loneliness and ‘evolutionary failure’ felt wildly disconnected from reality. The commenters were correct that I do have a cat, and he is good company; he even got an official acknowledgement on my thesis.

These personal circumstances made it easier to be resilient and sometimes laugh in the face of the vitriol. However, I kept thinking about how it might feel to receive comments like this as an undergraduate, as someone who is still uncertain about their life choices, or perhaps as someone simply having a bad day. That made me feel like I had a responsibility to push back, publicly and visibly, and to expose the absurdity of these narratives.

I sifted through sprawling threads of comments in which men (it was only men) speculated about my life choices, and selected the most revealing examples of misogynistic posts to highlight. In my responses, I alternated between humorous commentary and genuine explanations of my research.

Redirecting attention to my research

Because countless eyes were suddenly on my profile, I used the attention to introduce people to my work in the field of social evolution, including the insights that it can provide about the development of multicellularity. As one of my supervisors said, “get them in with the rage baiting, then give them biology.” Later that week, ‘rage bait’ became the Oxford University Press word of the year for 2025.

I took the opportunity to share one of my papers, which was published in Nature Ecology & Evolution in 2024. In it, my co-authors and I showed that larger colony sizes in ants enabled the evolution of a greater number of specialized worker castes1. This is part of my laboratory’s research into evolutionary patterns. The findings offer insights into how biological systems divide labour by delegating it to different groups of specialized cells — a process that made it possible for single cells to evolve into complex multicellular organisms.

Sharing our work with my newly engaged audience was met with enthusiasm and curiosity, and even some previously hostile commenters had to admit that this work was pretty cool. This inspired me to keep going, so I also shared a paper that my colleagues and I published this year in Evolution, which tackled similar ideas2.

Juliet Turner completed a PhD focused on cooperative insects and how they distribute labour.Credit: Juliet Turner

I posted my thesis title page too, and then my abstract, and I watched in disbelief as they were circulated around the Internet by haters and supporters alike. I wondered if I could send over my CV for worldwide distribution, too.

Solidarity between women in academia
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a real-world issue of online misogyny against women in academia, showing how the author transformed negative harassment into a positive platform to promote scientific research and challenge gender bias. The story has broad societal significance by addressing gender equality and resilience, and it provides detailed context about the author's experience and response.
articles.personality_title: PhD graduate turns online harassment into a chance to share her science
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Juliet Turner, a researcher at the University of Oxford, had just finished her PhD studying how insects work together. She posted a happy photo online to celebrate her achievement.

**What happened** – Instead of congratulations, Juliet faced harsh and unfair comments from strangers online. Many criticized her for being a woman with a PhD, making rude remarks about her life choices and personal future. The negative attention spread widely on social media.

**Impact** – Rather than staying silent, Juliet chose to respond by sharing the most extreme comments with humor and explaining her research. This caught people’s attention and helped many learn about her important work on how ants organize their colonies. Some critics even admitted the science was interesting.

**What's next step** – Juliet plans to keep using her online presence to share more about her research and encourage others, especially women in science. Her story shows how negative experiences can be turned into opportunities to educate and challenge unfair stereotypes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Juliet Turner transformed online attacks about her PhD into a powerful way to promote science and fight gender bias.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une doctorante transforme le harcèlement en ligne en une occasion de partager sa science
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Juliet Turner, chercheuse à l'Université d'Oxford, venait de terminer son doctorat sur la coopération des insectes. Elle a publié une photo joyeuse pour célébrer son succès.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Au lieu de félicitations, Juliet a reçu des commentaires durs et injustes en ligne. Beaucoup critiquaient son choix d'être une femme docteure, avec des remarques méchantes sur sa vie personnelle. L'attention négative s'est rapidement répandue sur les réseaux sociaux.

**Impact** – Plutôt que de rester silencieuse, Juliet a choisi de répondre en partageant les commentaires les plus extrêmes avec humour et en expliquant ses recherches. Cela a attiré l'attention et a permis à beaucoup de découvrir son travail important sur l'organisation des colonies de fourmis. Certains critiques ont même reconnu l'intérêt scientifique.

**Prochaines étapes** – Juliet compte continuer à utiliser sa présence en ligne pour partager ses recherches et encourager d'autres personnes, surtout les femmes en science. Son histoire montre comment des expériences négatives peuvent devenir des occasions d'apprendre et de lutter contre les stéréotypes.

**Phrase clé** – Juliet Turner a transformé les attaques en ligne sur son doctorat en un moyen puissant de promouvoir la science et de combattre les préjugés sexistes.
articles.personality_title_es: Una doctora convierte el acoso en línea en una oportunidad para compartir su ciencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Juliet Turner, investigadora en la Universidad de Oxford, acababa de terminar su doctorado sobre cómo cooperan los insectos. Publicó una foto feliz para celebrar su logro.

**Qué pasó** – En lugar de felicitaciones, Juliet recibió comentarios duros e injustos en línea. Muchas personas criticaron que fuera una mujer con un doctorado, haciendo comentarios groseros sobre su vida personal. La atención negativa se difundió rápidamente en las redes sociales.

**Impacto** – En vez de quedarse callada, Juliet decidió responder compartiendo los comentarios más extremos con humor y explicando su investigación. Esto llamó la atención y ayudó a que muchas personas conocieran su importante trabajo sobre cómo se organizan las colonias de hormigas. Algunos críticos incluso admitieron que la ciencia era interesante.

**Próximo paso** – Juliet planea seguir usando su presencia en línea para compartir más sobre su investigación y animar a otros, especialmente a mujeres en la ciencia. Su historia muestra cómo las experiencias negativas pueden convertirse en oportunidades para educar y desafiar estereotipos injustos.

**Frase clave** – Juliet Turner transformó los ataques en línea sobre su doctorado en una forma poderosa de promover la ciencia y luchar contra el sesgo de género.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a proud ant colony working together in intricate harmony inside a golden limestone archway resembling Oxford’s New College, with a glowing academic gown draped over a stone pedestal nearby and a content cat curled up peacefully at the base, all rendered in soft natural earth tones and gentle light.

articles.title: ‘No matter what’, UN Women vows to stay in Afghanistan, amid deepening rights crisis
articles.summary: UN Women will continue delivering for Afghan women and girls despite sweeping restrictions and ongoing instability, a senior official said on Tuesday.
articles.content:
The agency is operating in what it describes as the world’s most severe women’s rights crisis, where women are nearly four times less likely than men to access formal justice. Yet programmes remain active, reaching hundreds of thousands in need.

“Despite the restrictions on women and girls and the current conflicts…no matter what, UN Women is staying and delivering in Afghanistan,” said Susan Ferguson.

In 2025 alone, UN Women helped safeguard access to lifesaving services for more than 350,000 women and girls and supported nearly 200 women-led civil society organisations.

However, operations continue to face major constraints. In a country where services for women must be delivered by women, the agency is calling for the ban on Afghan female UN staff and contractors accessing UN premises to be lifted.

Tweet URL

“We continue to find ways to operate,” Ms. Ferguson said, “and stand with those who need support the most.”

Oppression intensifying

Ms. Ferguson emphasised the impact of Decree No. 12 issued by the Taliban earlier this year, which formally removes equality between men and women before the law and authorises violence against women by allowing husbands to carry out punishments, including physical violence, in the home.

“We call on the de facto authorities to ensure that laws and policies protect the rights of women and girls in line with Afghanistan's international human rights obligations”, the UN Women country representative said.

Ms. Ferguson warned the international community of “normalisation”.

“When we start to accept this as normal, we stop believing it can change...change is still possible, but only if the world continues to stand with Afghan women.”

Heavy toll of conflict

After an air strike in Kabul on Monday night reportedly killed hundreds of people, more than half of them women and children, Ms. Ferguson said “women and children are paying a heavy price for the continuing violence.”

At least 64,000 people have been affected by the military escalation with Pakistan, just over half of them women and girls.

Amid ongoing hostilities with Pakistan and conflict in the Middle East, “for many women, this is their second or even third time they've been forced to flee in the past year.”

She anticipates “a sharp increase in Afghans returning from Iran in the coming weeks, including women traveling alone or with children.”

With more than 10 million women and girls already expected to need humanitarian assistance this year following a deepening economic crisis and disruption caused by the August 2025 earthquake, “rising prices linked to conflict in the region will make it even harder for families.”

Urgent shelter needs

Initial assessments by the UN and partners show that women's most urgent needs are shelter, healthcare and clean water.

UN Women is also mobilising resources to expand cash support for women-headed households, provide well-being kits and help them start small businesses.

Despite these initiatives, UN Women faces a 50 per cent funding gap for their work in Afghanistan in 2026 with nearly $500 million still needed.

Calling on the international community, Ms Ferguson said, “we urgently need more funding to meet these needs at this critical moment... If the current hostilities continue, needs will only grow.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights UN Women's ongoing commitment to supporting Afghan women and girls amid severe rights restrictions and conflict, demonstrating significant positive real-world impact on a vulnerable population. It details substantial humanitarian aid reaching hundreds of thousands, addresses urgent needs, and calls for international support, reflecting meaningful scope and substance.
articles.personality_title: UN Women stays committed to Afghan women amid growing crisis
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Afghanistan is facing one of the worst crises for women’s rights in the world. Women are much less likely than men to access justice, and recent laws have allowed violence against women at home. The country also faces ongoing conflict and economic problems.

**What happened**
UN Women, a United Nations agency focused on gender equality and women’s rights, announced it will continue its work in Afghanistan despite these difficulties. In 2025, the agency helped over 350,000 women and girls access lifesaving services and supported nearly 200 women-led groups. However, they face challenges like restrictions on female staff entering UN offices.

**Impact**
UN Women’s work is crucial because many Afghan women rely on their programs for shelter, healthcare, clean water, and financial support. The agency is helping women start small businesses and providing emergency aid. This support reaches hundreds of thousands even as violence and strict rules make life harder.

**What's next step**
UN Women is calling for more international funding to continue and expand its programs. They also urge Afghan authorities to protect women’s rights according to international laws. The agency expects more Afghan women and children to need help soon due to conflict and displacement.

**One-sentence takeaway**
UN Women is determined to keep supporting Afghan women and girls despite growing challenges, providing vital aid and calling for global support to meet urgent needs.
articles.personality_title_fr: ONU Femmes reste engagée auprès des femmes afghanes malgré la crise croissante
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
L'Afghanistan traverse une des pires crises des droits des femmes au monde. Les femmes ont beaucoup moins accès à la justice que les hommes, et de nouvelles lois autorisent la violence domestique. Le pays souffre aussi de conflits et de problèmes économiques.

**Ce qui s'est passé**
ONU Femmes, une agence des Nations Unies pour l'égalité des sexes, a annoncé qu'elle continuerait son travail en Afghanistan malgré ces difficultés. En 2025, elle a aidé plus de 350 000 femmes et filles à accéder à des services vitaux et soutenu près de 200 groupes dirigés par des femmes. Mais des restrictions empêchent le personnel féminin d'entrer dans les locaux de l'ONU.

**Impact**
Le travail d'ONU Femmes est essentiel car beaucoup de femmes afghanes dépendent de leurs programmes pour un abri, des soins, de l'eau potable et un soutien financier. L'agence aide aussi les femmes à créer de petites entreprises et fournit une aide d'urgence. Ce soutien touche des centaines de milliers de personnes malgré la violence et les règles strictes.

**Prochaine étape**
ONU Femmes demande plus de financements internationaux pour continuer et élargir ses programmes. Elle appelle aussi les autorités afghanes à protéger les droits des femmes selon les lois internationales. L'agence prévoit que davantage de femmes et d'enfants auront besoin d'aide à cause des conflits et des déplacements.

**Phrase clé**
ONU Femmes reste déterminée à soutenir les femmes et filles afghanes malgré les défis, en fournissant une aide vitale et en appelant à un soutien mondial pour répondre aux besoins urgents.
articles.personality_title_es: ONU Mujeres mantiene su compromiso con las mujeres afganas en medio de la crisis creciente
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Afganistán enfrenta una de las peores crisis de derechos para las mujeres en el mundo. Las mujeres tienen mucho menos acceso a la justicia que los hombres, y nuevas leyes permiten la violencia en casa. El país también sufre conflictos y problemas económicos.

**Qué pasó**
ONU Mujeres, una agencia de las Naciones Unidas para la igualdad de género, anunció que seguirá trabajando en Afganistán a pesar de estas dificultades. En 2025, ayudó a más de 350,000 mujeres y niñas a acceder a servicios vitales y apoyó a casi 200 grupos liderados por mujeres. Sin embargo, enfrentan restricciones como la prohibición de que el personal femenino acceda a las oficinas de la ONU.

**Impacto**
El trabajo de ONU Mujeres es vital porque muchas mujeres afganas dependen de sus programas para obtener refugio, atención médica, agua limpia y apoyo financiero. También ayudan a mujeres a iniciar pequeños negocios y ofrecen ayuda de emergencia. Este apoyo llega a cientos de miles a pesar de la violencia y las reglas estrictas.

**Próximo paso**
ONU Mujeres pide más fondos internacionales para continuar y ampliar sus programas. También insta a las autoridades afganas a proteger los derechos de las mujeres según las leyes internacionales. Se espera que más mujeres y niños necesiten ayuda debido a los conflictos y desplazamientos.

**Frase clave**
ONU Mujeres está decidida a seguir apoyando a las mujeres y niñas afganas a pesar de los desafíos, proporcionando ayuda vital y llamando a la comunidad mundial a apoyar las necesidades urgentes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a sturdy, illuminated lantern held aloft by a group of silhouetted women figures standing together on a rugged Afghan mountain landscape at dusk, symbolizing resilience and hope amid darkness, with soft earth tones and warm golden light radiating from the lantern, surrounded by simple representations of water vessels and small shelters nearby, conveying shelter, support, and community solidarity.
